attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Original rhyming verse retellings of world myths through the Alice frame. Each poem is faithful to its source myth — the stories are real, the rhymes are ours, the wonder belongs to the traditions. Alice is a guest in every world she visits. She does not explain; she listens, stumbles, and is changed.

attribution.name: Lewis Carroll
attribution.note: The Alice frame — a curious girl who falls into other worlds — is borrowed from Carroll's Alice's Adventures in Wonderland (public domain). The nonsense-with-teeth tone is his gift to us.

attribution.name: Source Traditions
attribution.note: Haida/Tlingit (Raven), Akan/Ashanti (Anansi), Inuit (Sedna), Shinto/Japanese (Amaterasu), K'iche' Maya (Hero Twins), Pan-North American Indigenous (Coyote), Sumerian (Inanna), Osage/Cherokee (Creation). Each poem links to its source grammar in this repository.

items.name: Ep1 Scene 1: Canto 0 — The Falling (or: How Alice Found a Book That Found Her Back)

sections.Narration:
Alice sat in a library chair
With sensible shoes and sensible hair,
Reading a book about myths of the world —
When the pages fluttered and crackled and curled.

'That's odd,' said Alice, 'I didn't turn that.'
The book disagreed. It opened up flat
And wider, and wider, and wider still,
Till the words fell off like snow from a hill.

sections.Narration:
The letters rearranged on the floor
Into shapes that she'd never quite seen before:
A raven, a spider, a serpent of light,
A coyote that winked and slipped out of sight.

'Books aren't supposed to do that,' she said.
'Neither are girls,' said a voice from the spread
Of pages now forming a kind of a hole —
A tunnel of stories that swallowed her whole.

sections.Narration:
She fell past the contents, the index, the spine,
Past footnotes that whispered, 'You're doing just fine,'
Past margins where someone had scribbled in red:
'THE MYTHS ARE ALIVE. THE READER IS READ.'

And Alice, who always was partial to falling
(It's better, she'd found, than sitting or crawling),
Let go of the chair and the sensible shoes
And fell into stories she didn't quite choose —

sections.Narration:
Or rather, the stories had chosen her first.
They do that, you know. It's the very worst
Habit of myths: they don't wait to be read.
They reach through the page and they pull you instead.

So here she goes tumbling through ink and through time,
And what happens next — well, it happens in rhyme.

items.name: Ep2 Scene 1: Canto I — Raven's Smoke Hole (or: How Light Got Loose)

sections.Narration:
Alice landed in darkness. Proper dark.
Not the dark of a closet or after-hours park,
But the dark before anything — thick and complete,
The kind you could stand on with both of your feet.

'Excuse me,' she said to the nothing around,
'Is there anyone here?' Not a peep. Not a sound.
Then a voice like a laugh wrapped in feathers and rain:
'There's me. And I have a MAGNIFICENT PLAN.'

Raven appeared — or she felt that he did.
He was black in the blackness, expertly hid,
With an eye like a coal and a beak like a grin
And the look of someone about to begin
Something naughty.

items.name: Ep2 Scene 2: Canto I — Raven's Smoke Hole (or: How Light Got Loose)

sections.Narration:
'You see,' Raven said with a caw,
'An old chief keeps the light locked in a drawer —
Well, three boxes, actually. Sun, Moon, and Stars.
He hoards them like treasure. He's locked them in jars.
The whole world is dark because one greedy fellow
Won't share what he's got. Sound familiar? Oh well, oh —
I've tried asking nicely. I've tried being rude.
I've tried being charming. I've tried being crude.
So now I shall try being VERY SMALL.'

And Raven — who wasn't exactly small —
Shrunk himself down to a needle of pine,
A tiny green sliver, impossibly fine,
And dropped — plip! — in the cup of the old chief's daughter.

She drank. She didn't think she oughter
Have swallowed a trickster, but there you go.
These things happen more often than people know.

items.name: Ep2 Scene 3: Canto I — Raven's Smoke Hole (or: How Light Got Loose)

sections.Narration:
Nine months later: a baby. Dark-eyed, dark-skinned,
Who SCREAMED like the world's most furious wind.
Nothing would stop him — no rocking, no song —
He wailed and he wailed and he wailed all day long
Till Grandfather said, 'Give the child what he wants!'

(Grandparents are like that. They do what aunts
And uncles and parents would never permit.
Grandfathers especially. They spoil, a bit.)

The baby got the star-box. Opened the lid.
The stars flew up through the smoke hole — they did! —
And scattered themselves across all of the sky
Like someone had sneezed a great glittering sigh.

sections.Narration:
Alice watched them appear. 'Oh!' she said. 'Oh!'
The baby just grinned and continued his show.
He SCREAMED for the moon-box. Got that one too.
The moon sailed up silver and perfectly new.

Then came the sun-box — the last, and the best.
Grandfather clutched it against his old chest
But the baby-who-wasn't-a-baby transformed —
Back into Raven! Black-feathered and stormed
With laughter, he grabbed it and flew for the smoke hole,
That gap in the ceiling, that crack, that one whole
Opening between the dark house and the sky —

He burst through. He opened the box. He could fly.

sections.Narration:
Raven shrugged. 'Was it free
Before he locked it up? Was light his to keep?
I stole what was stolen. Now go back to sleep —
Or rather, wake up. That's the trick of the light:
You don't make it. You just stop hoarding the night.'

Alice blinked in the sudden, ridiculous sun.
'That was very dishonest,' she said.

'Wasn't it fun?'
Said Raven, and laughed, and became a black dot
In the sky, getting smaller.

It was. Quite a lot.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: Raven is not good or evil — he is appetite. He steals the light not to help humanity but because he wants it. Helping everyone is the side effect. This is the trickster's deepest teaching: transformation comes through desire, not virtue. The Haida, Tlingit, and Tsimshian have told this story for thousands of years. Bill Reid and Robert Bringhurst's literary retelling is widely known. Raven's theft shares structure with Prometheus but replaces noble sacrifice with hungry cunning.

items.name: Ep3 Scene 1: Canto II — Anansi's Golden Chest (or: How Stories Got Their Legs)

sections.Narration:
The light from Raven's sun was still brand new
When Alice fell again (as Alices do)
And landed in a web — a vast, vast web
That stretched from here to there and back, each thread
A shining line that hummed a different song.

'Don't struggle,' said a voice. 'It won't take long.
I'm Anansi. I'm small. I'm rather clever.
And I'm about to do what no one's ever
Managed before. Come watch. You'll want to see this.'

A spider. Not a large one. Rather, he is
The opposite of large — the size of THOUGHT
Before it's thought, a tiny tangled knot
Of legs and eyes and PLANS.

items.name: Ep3 Scene 2: Canto II — Anansi's Golden Chest (or: How Stories Got Their Legs)

sections.Narration:
'What are you doing?' Alice asked the spider.
'I'm climbing up to God to be a buyer
Of STORIES. Nyame — that's the Sky God's name —
Keeps every tale locked up. The world's a frame
Without a picture, a mouth without a word.
No story's ever told. No tale is heard.
Because He hoards them in a golden chest
Beside his throne. I think that's — well — not best.'

'And what's the price?' asked Alice.

sections.Narration:
'Oh, not much.
Just four impossible things. You know, and such.
Onini the Python — I must capture him.
The Hornets of Mmoboro — on a whim.
Osebo the Leopard — he's rather fierce.
And the Invisible Fairy — try to pierce
THAT veil! Kings have tried. Warriors have failed.
The mightiest heroes wept and wailed
And went home empty-handed.'

'So what will you do?'

sections.Narration:
Anansi grinned (all eight eyes shining through).
'What I always do. I'll THINK.'

For the python, he argued loud and long
With his wife: 'The python's short!' 'No, he's strong
And LONG!' They bickered till Onini came
To settle it himself (a python's vain
About his length). 'Stretch out beside this stick!'
Onini did — and Anansi, quick-quick-quick,
Tied him up. 'Thanks for measuring!' he said.

For the hornets, he poured rain on his head
And theirs: 'Oh no! A storm! Quick, hide inside
This lovely dry gourd!' The hornets complied.
He plugged the hole.

'Well,' said Nyame. 'I honestly think
No one has EVER — you're a SPIDER. You're SMALL.'

items.name: Ep3 Scene 6: Canto II — Anansi's Golden Chest (or: How Stories Got Their Legs)

sections.Narration:
'Being small,' said Anansi, 'is no problem at all.
Being stupid — now THAT is a problem. The chest?'

Nyame opened it.

And out flew the rest
Of everything: stories and fables and songs,
Nursery rhymes and the rights and the wrongs
Of a thousand generations told by the fire.
They scattered like starlings, higher and higher,
Till every village and every home
Had stories to tell and no one was alone
At night without a tale to hold.

'And THAT,' said Anansi, 'is why they're called
Spider-stories — Anansesem — every one.
Not because I'm the hero. I'm not. I'm the pun.
I'm the twist in the plot. The knot in the thread.
I'm the reason you're listening instead
Of sleeping. I'm the itch that won't be scratched.
I'm the egg that shouldn't have been hatched
But was, and now — well — HERE WE ALL ARE.'

Alice looked at the web stretching out, near and far.
'You tricked everyone,' she said.

'I set them free.
The python got famous. The hornets agree
It's a very good story. The leopard's still cross
But even he admits it's no great loss
To be part of the tale that FREED ALL THE TALES.'

And Anansi climbed off on his silvery rails
To spin one more story. He always does.
That's the thing about tricksters: they are what they was
And they was what they is — which is grammar gone wrong,
But grammar gone wrong is the start of a song.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: Anansi crossed the Atlantic in the holds of slave ships. Carried by Akan, Ashanti, and Fante peoples through the Middle Passage, he survived in Jamaica, Suriname, Curaçao, and the Gullah/Geechee communities of the American South. He became a figure of resistance — the enslaved person's model for surviving through wit when strength was impossible. Bayo Akomolafe reads Anansi as the crack in the system, the compost-figure who thrives in the margins of power. R.S. Rattray collected these tales in Akan-Ashanti Folk-Tales (1930).

sections.Narration:
The web dissolved. The stories blew away.
Alice fell through cold — a different day,
A different dark, but this one had a bite.
The air was sharp. The water black as night.

She landed on an ice floe, blue and wide,
With nothing but the Arctic on each side,
And in the distance — cutting through the freeze —
A kayak, riding impossible seas.

Inside: a girl. A girl who would not marry.

items.name: Ep4 Scene 2: Canto III — Sedna's Fingers (or: Where the Seals Come From)

sections.Narration:
'I'm Sedna,' said the girl. 'And I won't carry
Anyone's name but mine. My father tried
To give me to this man, and that. I cried
Not tears of sadness — tears of NO. And no.
And no again. I didn't want to go
With anyone. Is that so hard to see?'

Alice nodded. 'I understand,' said she.
'I once refused to play a game of croquet
With a queen. It didn't go well.'

'One day,'
Said Sedna, 'a stranger came. He was handsome.
His furs were fine. He seemed a prince — a ransom
Of beauty. I went with him. Across the water.'

sections.Narration:
Her voice got quiet. Like a caught otter
Going still.

'He wasn't what he seemed.
He was a bird-spirit. A fulmar. I dreamed
Of fine things. What I got: a nest of fish skins
On a barren rock. No home. No kin.
I wept until my father came by kayak,
And I thought — I'm saved. My father. My daybreak.

But when the bird-spirit raised a storm
That rocked the boat and changed the water's form
To mountains made of waves and teeth of foam —
My father chose his life. He chose to go home.

items.name: Ep4 Scene 6: Canto III — Sedna's Fingers (or: Where the Seals Come From)

sections.Narration:
'Yes. My fingers grew
Into everything that feeds the Inuit. Through
My wound, the ocean fills. I sank below
To the sea floor, where the coldest currents flow,
And there I sit — Nuliajuk — Mother
Of every seal and whale. There is no other
Source. The animals come from my hands,
From what was taken, from the broken strands
Of trust between a daughter and her father.'

The ice floe rocked. The Arctic held its laughter.

'But here's the thing,' said Sedna from the deep.
'When humans waste the meat, or fail to keep
Their promises to what they kill and eat,
My hair gets tangled. Filthy. Full of sleet
And broken things. And then I hold them back —
The seals, the whales — I send them down the crack
Between the living and the merely fed.
And everything goes hungry. Until the thread
Is mended. Till the shaman comes below
And combs my hair, so gently, strand by slow
Strand — till the tangles smooth and I can feel
Something like kindness. Then I let the seal
Return. The whale. The walrus. All of it.
The whole ocean opens, lit by lit.'

Alice sat on the ice, her fingers cold.
She looked at them — all ten — and felt how old
This story was, and how it had no end.

'Every meal,' said Sedna, 'has a friend
Who suffered for it. Every bite you take
Arrived because something learned to break
And didn't stop becoming. That's the cost.
My fingers weren't lost.
They were planted.'

The ice floe cracked. Alice slipped through.
Below: the dark water. Above: the blue.
Between: the seals, the whales, the walrus, swimming
In circles, endless circles, always brimming
With something that felt like a severed grace —
Generous, wounded, feeding every place.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: Sedna is the most important deity in Inuit religion — the one on whom all life depends. Her story is not a hero myth but an origin-of-suffering myth: everything that feeds us comes from a wound, an original betrayal. Collected by Franz Boas (1888) and Knud Rasmussen (1929) from Central Inuit communities. Multiple variants exist across the Arctic. The shaman's act of combing Sedna's hair is one of the most beautiful images of ecological repair in any mythology.

items.name: Ep5 Scene 1: Canto IV — The Cave of Laughter (or: How to Cure a Sun Goddess)

sections.Narration:
Alice rose from water into air
And found herself — wet, cold, and everywhere
Surprised — in a country made of green:
Rice paddies, mountains, the loveliest scene
She'd ever landed in. Except — no light.
The sun was gone. It was perpetual night.

'Not AGAIN,' said Alice. 'We just fixed that.
Raven sorted it out, with a baby and a — '

'Different problem,' said a voice. Eight hundred,
Actually. Eight hundred voices thundered
And whispered and argued and fretted at once.
A parliament of gods (divine response
Teams aren't as organized as you'd expect).

items.name: Ep5 Scene 2: Canto IV — The Cave of Laughter (or: How to Cure a Sun Goddess)

sections.Narration:
'Amaterasu — Sun Goddess — has checked
Herself into a cave,' explained a small god
With a clipboard. 'The Rock Cave. Very odd.
She's sealed the door. She won't come out. We've tried
Everything — prayers, roosters, bribes. She cried
About her brother. Susanoo. The worst.
He wrecked her rice paddies — destroyed them first —
Then fouled her palace, killed her weaving-maid
By hurling a dead horse. Oh yes, he played
Quite rough, the storm god. Lightning's his toy.
So she went dark. And here we are. No joy.
No crops. No warmth. Evil spirits multiply.
The whole world's dying and we don't know why
She won't come back. We know WHY she went in.
We just can't think how to make her begin
Again.'

Alice considered this. 'Have you tried flowers?'

items.name: Ep5 Scene 6: Canto IV — The Cave of Laughter (or: How to Cure a Sun Goddess)

sections.Narration:
'We don't dare.'

Then out of nowhere — WACKY
And wonderful and absolutely mad —
Ame-no-Uzume (the best name that a god has had)
Overturned a washtub, climbed on top,
And started dancing. She would not stop.
She danced like nobody was watching — wrong.
Eight hundred gods were watching. Then, before long,
She pulled her robes apart. She laughed out loud.
She danced a dance so silly, wild, and proud
That every single god began to roar
With laughter — HUGE laughter — shaking the floor
Of heaven, rattling mountains, giggling clouds.
Eight hundred gods in hysterical crowds.

Inside the cave, Amaterasu heard.
And thought: 'That's very strange. How quite absurd.
The world is dark and everyone should weep.
But they're LAUGHING. That's — I cannot sleep
Through THAT.' She cracked the door. Just to look.

Outside, the gods had placed (by hook and crook)
A mirror. Amaterasu saw — her face.
Her radiance. Her light filling the space.
She'd never seen herself before. 'That's — ME?'
She reached toward the mirror. 'Can that be — ?'

A strong god grabbed her. Pulled her from the cave.
They sealed it shut. The world got back its brave
Bright sun. The rice grew. Evil spirits fled.
The darkness ended. And you know what led
Her out?

Not duty. Not a hero's might.
Not a weapon or a prayer or a fight.

Laughter. Joy. A woman dancing free.
And a mirror that said: 'Look. That light is thee.'

Alice clapped her hands. 'That's the best one yet!
The cure for darkness isn't war — it's a brunette
On a washtub doing something rather rude!'

The eight hundred gods, in grateful mood,
Nodded solemnly. 'We thought so too.
There's very little that a dance can't do.'

And Alice, laughing, fell through the mirror's gleam
Into the next myth, and the next, and the next dream.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: This is the only major sun-withdrawal myth in which the sun is female and the solution is not heroic battle but comedy and eros. Amaterasu is not rescued — she rescues herself, drawn out by curiosity and the reflection of her own beauty. Translated by Basil Hall Chamberlain (1882, public domain). Campbell noted its structural similarity to Demeter's mourning but with a radically different resolution — not grief satisfied but laughter provoked. The mirror is central to Shinto worship to this day.

items.name: Ep6 Scene 1: Canto V — The Ball Game Below (or: How to Beat Death at His Own Sport)

sections.Narration:
The mirror shattered into seven stones
And Alice fell through earth and roots and bones
Into a staircase spiraling below,
Where strange cold fires cast a greenish glow
And someone — two someones — were bouncing a ball.

'Hello!' said the first. 'You're just in time to fall
With US. I'm Hunahpu. This is my twin,
Xbalanque. We're about to begin
The greatest game of ball you've ever seen.
Except the other team is rather mean.
They're DEATH.'

'Death plays ball?' asked Alice.

items.name: Ep6 Scene 2: Canto V — The Ball Game Below (or: How to Beat Death at His Own Sport)

sections.Narration:
'Death plays EVERYTHING,'
Said Xbalanque. 'That's the thing with him. Or them.
The Lords of Xibalba — that's the underworld —
They hate it when we play above. They hurled
A summons at our father once. He went.
He didn't come back. Down the long descent
He went, and they killed him. Then our uncle too.
So now it's us. And here's what we must do:'

They counted on their fingers:
'The Dark House first —
Total darkness, no light, you'll be immersed
In nothing. Then the Razor House (quite bleak).
The Cold House (bring a sweater for the week).
The Jaguar House (self-explanatory, that).
The Fire House (hot). The Bat House (bring a hat.
Actually, bring a HEAD. You'll understand).'

'This doesn't sound like fun,' said Alice, fanned
By something cold that moved without a breeze.

items.name: Ep6 Scene 3: Canto V — The Ball Game Below (or: How to Beat Death at His Own Sport)

sections.Narration:
'It's NOT fun,' said Hunahpu. 'But if you please —
The point's not fun. The point is: DON'T BE STRONG.
Be clever. Every hero gets it wrong
Who tries to fight the Lords of Death head-on.
You can't defeat death. Death is never gone.
But you CAN trick him. Watch.'

They lit cigars
With fireflies (not fire) and used the stars
On macaw tails for torches in the dark —
Things that LOOKED like what Death asked. Each spark
Of cleverness got them through one more room.

But in the Bat House — gloom on bitter gloom —
A great bat swooped and took Hunahpu's head
Clean off. His body crumpled. Alice said,
'He's DEAD! His head is — '

sections.Narration:
'A squash. The Lords won't have a clue.
You'd be amazed what death can't tell apart.
He's very literal. He has no art.'

The squash-headed twin and his brother played on.
They beat the Death Lords at their game. Then — gone!
They pulled their GREATEST trick:

They killed each other.
Each twin murdered his brother.
Then brought each other back. Alive. Just fine.
The Lords of Death applauded. 'THAT'S divine!
Do that to US! Kill US and bring us back!'

items.name: Ep6 Scene 6: Canto V — The Ball Game Below (or: How to Beat Death at His Own Sport)

sections.Narration:
'Of course!' the twins said. Crack. And crack. And crack.
They killed the Death Lords. Every single one.
Then dusted off their hands.

'We're done.'

'But — aren't you going to bring them back?' said Alice.

'No,' said Hunahpu, grinning without malice
(Well, maybe a little). 'They asked to die.
We obliged. We just forgot the second part. Oh my.'

The twins rose upward — through the stone, the roots,
The earth, the grass — like two bright golden shoots —
And burst into the sky.

One became the sun.
One became the moon.

And Alice, stunned,
Watched them rise and thought: the cleverest thing
About these twins was this — they didn't bring
Weapons to the underworld. They brought a BALL.
A game. A squash. The nerve. The gall.
The absolute CHEEK of playing games with Death
And winning — not with swords but with your breath
Held steady while you carve a face from fruit
And act like nothing's wrong. Like it's a hoot.

'THAT,' said Alice, 'is the bravest thing I've seen.'

And the moon winked.
And the sun turned slightly green
(Being, after all, a twin who'd lost his head
And got a squash instead.)

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: The Popol Vuh is the most complete surviving pre-Columbian creation narrative. Dennis Tedlock's translation (1985) is the standard English version. The Hero Twins' victory over death comes through trickery and performance, not heroic combat. The ball game was a real Mayan ritual — a reenactment of cosmic struggle played on stone courts throughout Mesoamerica. The squash-head is one of the great images in world mythology: you can replace what was taken with what grows from the earth.

items.name: Ep7 Scene 2: Canto VI — Coyote's Stars (or: Why the Sky Is Such a Mess)

sections.Narration:
'HA HA HA HA!'

A laugh — but sideways. Crooked. Full of grit.
A coyote stood there. Grinning. Every bit
As scruffy as you'd think. One ear was bent.
His tail was matted. And he clearly went
Wherever he pleased and pleased wherever he went.

'I'm Coyote!' he announced (as if the name
Were news). 'And EVERYTHING is my game.
I stole fire from the Fire People — ONCE.
I raced a river — lost — because I'm a dunce
At anything that's straight. I challenged a rock
To a footrace. (Don't.) I tried to unlock
The secret of flight by tying birds to my arms.
I fell. I've died — oh, seven times? — on farms,
In rivers, off cliffs, inside a whale,
From eating my own tail (a cautionary tale),
And once from sheer embarrassment. But look:
I'm here! You can't kill Coyote. Close the book
And I'll be on the next page. Burn the page
And I'll be in the ash. There is no cage
That holds me, no death that sticks, no night
So dark I won't show up to steal the — '

Splashed across the sky like spilled milk — SPLAT!

items.name: Ep7 Scene 5: Canto VI — Coyote's Stars (or: Why the Sky Is Such a Mess)

sections.Narration:
'Look up,' he said. 'Look at that glorious joint
Of scattered, random, messy, BEAUTIFUL light.
Is it organized? No. Is it right?
It's BETTER than right. It's ALIVE. Every star
Is where it fell, not where the planners are.
The Milky Way? That's where I spilled the most.
The gods wanted a spreadsheet. I gave them toast
With the jam side down — and the jam side down
Is ALWAYS the side that feeds the ground.'

Alice looked up. The sky was wild and wide
And nothing was where nothing should reside
And everything was everywhere at once.

'You're a menace,' she said.

items.name: Ep7 Scene 6: Canto VI — Coyote's Stars (or: Why the Sky Is Such a Mess)

sections.Narration:
'I'm a dunce,'
Said Coyote, 'who happens to be right
About ONE thing: the prettiest kind of light
Is the kind that nobody planned.
I died for that. Several times. And
I'll die for it again. But not today.
Today I have more mischief. Run away
With me!'

'Where?'

'Wherever the story goes!'

And Coyote ran — on his crooked toes,
Through the sagebrush, over the moon-white sand,
And Alice ran after, not quite as planned,
Because nothing with Coyote is planned at all —
Which is why, when you trip, it's a different kind of fall.

The useful kind.
The kind where you land
In a story you never meant
To understand.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: Coyote is not a god, not a hero, not a villain — he is the principle of ongoing disruption. Paul Radin (The Trickster, 1956) saw Coyote as the earliest mythic figure: older than the hero, older than the god, the original consciousness before it learned to be serious. Jung wrote the commentary to Radin's book. Barry Lopez's Giving Birth to Thunder, Sleeping with His Daughter (1977) is an accessible collection. Coyote stories were told by Navajo, Crow, Blackfoot, Nez Perce, Klamath, Paiute, Maidu, and many other nations. Bayo Akomolafe reads the trickster as the mythic embodiment of the crack — the place where the system fails and something new leaks through.

items.name: Ep8 Scene 1: Canto VII — The Seven Gates (or: What You Lose on the Way Down)

sections.Narration:
Coyote vanished. Sand became stone.
Stone became staircase. Alice, alone,
Descended into earth that smelled of clay
And copper and the oldest kind of day —
The kind before there WERE days. Sumerian.
Four thousand years before the Guardian
Newspaper. Five thousand before the Tweet.
Six thousand before anyone's cold feet
About commitment.

At the first gate: a woman.
Not just a woman. Something more than human.
Inanna. Queen of Heaven AND of Earth,
Goddess of Love, of War, of Death, of Birth —
Dressed in EVERYTHING. Crown of lapis blue.
Gold breastplate. Necklace. Ring. One royal shoe
(The other was a backup). Measuring rod.
She looked like what you'd get if every god
Decided to be ONE person for a day.

'Where are you going?' Alice dared to say.

But first — remove your crown.'

'My crown? Alas —
Why must I?'

sections.Narration:
The second gate:
'Remove your necklace.' Inanna, irate
But willing, took it off. The lapis beads
Fell scattering like small blue seeds.

The third gate: breastplate.
Fourth: her golden ring.
Fifth: the measuring rod — her claim to EVERYTHING
She'd measured, counted, ruled.
Sixth: her robe.
Seventh: the last cloth on her body. Globe
Of power, stripped to nothing. Naked. Bare.
She stood before her sister's throne. Down THERE,
In the lowest room, where no light has ever been,
Ereshkigal — the other queen —
Looked at her naked sister.

And killed her.

items.name: Ep8 Scene 6: Canto VII — The Seven Gates (or: What You Lose on the Way Down)

sections.Narration:
'Keep watching,' said a voice from the dead air.

Above, the world began to die.
No grain grew. Animals wouldn't multiply.
The bees stopped buzzing. Flowers wouldn't bloom.
The world became a vast and sterile room
Because the goddess of EVERYTHING alive
Was dead below, and nothing could survive
Without her.

Three days later, Inanna's servant —
Clever, faithful, absolutely fervent —
Sent two small mourners to the underworld.
They found the body, still and stiff and curled,
And sprinkled her with water, bread of life.
She woke. She rose. But the underworld's knife
Has a rule: you don't leave without a substitute.
Someone must stay. No question. Absolute.

Inanna climbed back up — through seven gates,
Receiving back her clothes, her crown, her weights
And measures — back to the world above.
And found her husband, Dumuzi. And love?
He sat upon her throne. Comfortable. Fed.
Not mourning. Not a single tear he'd shed.

She pointed at him.

'Take him.'

Down he went.
Six months below, six months above. He spent
Half a life in darkness for the crime
Of sitting pretty in her throne. And time —
The seasons — winter, summer — that's HIS turn:
Below when nothing grows. Above when ferns
Uncurl and wheat comes up and rivers flood.
He pays the debt. In darkness and in mud.

Alice sat on the ancient stair and thought.
'She went down naked. She fought
Nothing. She won nothing. She just — WENT.
And gave up everything. And what it meant — '

'Was this,' said Inanna, shining, dressed again:
'You cannot know the underworld through men
Or weapons or plans. You go. You strip. You die.
And what comes back is different. That is why
I went. Not to defeat my sister. Not
To win. To learn the only thing you've got
When everything is taken:

You've got nothing.
And nothing — as it turns out — is something.'

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: This is the ancestor of all descent myths — older than Persephone by over a thousand years. Inanna goes voluntarily, not as a victim. She chooses to face death, and the price of return is that death gets someone. The Sumerian cuneiform tablets were recovered from ancient libraries and translated in the 19th-20th centuries. The seven gates stripping away identity prefigure every initiation rite in every culture: to be reborn, you must first be stripped of everything you think you are.

sections.Narration:
Alice rose from Sumer into sky —
Not sky below but sky UP HIGH,
The kind of sky you live in if you're Osage
And nobody has figured out the passage
Down to earth yet, because the earth is WATER.
Everything below is flood. The daughter
Of nothing, going nowhere. Just: wet.

'From where did we come?' the Osage asked. And yet
No one knew. So they went to Sun.

'You are my children,' Sun said. 'Every one.'

sections.Narration:
They wandered further. Found Moon.

'I'm your mother,' Moon said. 'Sun is your dad.
You have to leave here. I know it's sad,
But you must go DOWN. To the earth. And live.'

So down they came, with everything to give
And nowhere to give it — because the earth
Was water, wall to wall, and all the worth
Of all the Osage and all their kin
Meant nothing if they couldn't BEGIN
To find dry ground.

sections.Narration:
They floated. And they wept.
No answer came. The water kept.
They called to every god. Silence. Nope.
But all the animals were there. And hope
Is sometimes just an elk.

'Elk was the finest,' Alice heard a voice
Relate. 'The stateliest. Everyone's first choice.
They called to Elk. And Elk — magnificent,
With antlers like a forest — Elk just went
Down into the water. And began to sink.

But Elk did not panic. Elk did not think
Small thoughts. Elk called to the WINDS.

sections.Narration:
The winds came.

They blew across the water — not a game
But a MAKING. They blew and blew until
The water shrank, the edges fell, the still
Deep places drained, and there — like a brown back
Rising from a bath — the earth cracked
Through. Rocks appeared. And mud. And then
The softest thing: dry ground. Amen.
Or rather: the Osage didn't say amen.
They said something older. They said:
"The earth is here.
And we are on it.
And everything is clear."'

Alice stood on the new-made ground and felt
It under her feet — warm, solid, smelt
Of rain and roots and the underside of stones.

items.name: Ep9 Scene 6: Canto VIII — Elk Calls the Wind (or: How the Osage Found the Ground)

sections.Narration:
All the animals lived in the sky, you see,
In Galun'lati — crowded as can be.
They wanted room. So little Water Beetle,
Grandchild of Beaver — small, complete, and fetal
With curiosity — dove down beneath.
She couldn't find bottom. No floor. No reef.
But she brought up soft mud from somewhere deep
And it grew, and it grew, till the islands steep
Rose dripping from the sea. And Buzzard flew
Across the wet new world — and where he flew
Too low and his wings hit mud: the valleys fell.
Where he swept up: mountains. You can tell
Where Buzzard got tired — that's where the Great Smokies
Rise and fall like a sleeping giant who's cozy
Under a quilt of rhododendron and pine.'

Alice laughed. 'So the mountains are the sign
Of a tired bird's wings?'

'Everything that's big,'
Said the Cherokee voice, 'started with a dig
From something small. Water Beetle made the ground.
Buzzard shaped the mountains. All around,
The smallest creatures did the largest things.
Remember that when someone says that kings
Made the world. They didn't. Beetles did.
And everything that's grand was once well hid
In something very, very, very small.'

Alice knelt and touched the earth. That's all.
Just touched it. And felt grateful. And felt found.
The oldest prayer is just: to touch the ground.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: These creation stories come from two distinct nations — the Osage (Siouan-speaking people of the Great Plains) and the Cherokee (Iroquoian-speaking people of the Southeast). Both are recorded in early ethnographic collections now in the public domain. The Osage story emphasizes Elk's role as the stateliest animal who calls the winds — creation through nobility and appeal to natural forces. The Cherokee story emphasizes the smallest creatures: Water Beetle and Buzzard shape the entire landscape. Together they teach that creation comes from both the grand and the humble.

items.name: Ep10 Scene 1: Canto IX — The First Fire (or: Who Had the Nerve to Carry It)

sections.Narration:
The ground was made. The mountains rose. But still
The world was cold. A universal chill.
No fire. No warmth. No way to cook a fish
Or warm your hands or make a birthday wish
On candles (they hadn't been invented yet).

'The Thunders,' Alice heard, 'had placed a bet
Of sorts — they'd put fire in a tree.
A hollow sycamore, across the sea,
On an island. You could see the smoke
Rising from the top. But — here's the joke —
Nobody could get across the water.
The animals held council. Every daughter
And every son of every beast alive
Debated how to cross. How to survive
The swim and grab the fire and bring it back.'

Raven went first. Bold and proud and black,
He flew across the water, far and strong,
And landed on the sycamore. But long
Before he figured out what he should do,
The heat had scorched his feathers through and through.
He flew back, terrified. Still black. Still scared.
No fire.

items.name: Ep10 Scene 2: Canto IX — The First Fire (or: Who Had the Nerve to Carry It)

sections.Narration:
The Screech Owl went. She dared
The smoke. She peered into the hollow trunk.
A blast of heat — her eyes nearly sunk —
And left white rings around them, burned and bright.
She fled back home. She's had those rings of white
Around her eyes forever since that night.

The Hooting Owl tried next. Same story, friend.
The ashes burned his eyes. And in the end
He too flew home with circles singed and sore.
The Horned Owl? Same. His feathers evermore
Have ashy tufts where the fire bit.

The little snakes went next. They didn't quit
But the fire scorched them so their heads turned black.
The Blacksnake never did get his color back.

items.name: Ep10 Scene 4: Canto IX — The First Fire (or: Who Had the Nerve to Carry It)

sections.Narration:
She said: "I'll go."

The animals laughed. A spider? Ho ho ho.
She's TINY. She can't carry fire. She's wet.
She's a SPIDER. She's the lamest bet
In all of Cherokee creation.

But she spun a little bowl — a small formation
Of web, a tusti — on her back. A cup.
And she ran across the water (spiders run up
And over things that bigger creatures can't).
She reached the sycamore. And with a plant
Of her tiny legs, she put one single coal
Inside her tusti bowl.

items.name: Ep10 Scene 6: Canto IX — The First Fire (or: Who Had the Nerve to Carry It)

sections.Narration:
Not a burn. Not a scorch. Not a single chafe.

The world got warm.
The world got FIRE.

Not from the bravest
Or the one who flew the highest
Or the biggest
Or the mightiest —

From a spider
With a bowl on her back
Who simply thought:
I'll make a basket.
And I'll carry what I can.

'Oh,' said Alice, sitting by the brand-new flames,
Warming her hands. 'I know their names —
Raven, Owl, Snake — they tried with strength and size.
But the spider — she was WISE.
Not strong. Not fierce. Just: practical. And small.
And small, it seems, can carry most of all.'

The fire crackled. Cherokee night fell.
And Alice, warm for once, thought: well, well, well.
Beetles make the earth. Spiders bring the flame.
And every time — every single time — the game
Is won by someone no one thought could play.

She held her hands up to the fire and stayed
A while. You don't rush past a fire, she'd learned.
You sit. You warm. You honor what was earned
By something very small that didn't quit
When all the big brave beautiful things split.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: This Cherokee origin story explains both how fire came to the world and why certain animals look the way they do — Raven's black feathers, Screech Owl's eye-rings, Blacksnake's dark head. The story's genius is its structure: each attempt is grander and more dramatic, and each fails. The smallest creature succeeds not through courage but through craft — she weaves a container. The lesson is architectural, not heroic: the right vessel matters more than the right muscles.

items.name: Ep11 Scene 1: Canto X — The Waking (or: How Alice Came Back Different)

sections.Narration:
Alice sat in a library chair.
Same chair. Same shoes. Same sensible hair.
The book was closed. The pages still. The light
Came in through ordinary windows. Right.

But something had changed. Or rather: SHE had changed.
Her fingers felt — not different — rearranged.
As if they'd been, quite briefly, seals and whales
And walruses. As if the fingernails
Had held a coal of fire, small and bright.
As if they'd helped a spider through the night.

items.name: Ep11 Scene 2: Canto X — The Waking (or: How Alice Came Back Different)

sections.Narration:
She opened the book again. The myths were there —
Flat on the page. Just ink. Just words. Just air.
But now she KNEW things that you cannot know
From reading. Things you learn from down below.

She knew that Raven steals because he's HUNGRY
And hunger is how light gets free.
She knew that Anansi — small, absurd —
Owns every story. Every word
You've ever told around a fire at night
Has spider-legs. And that is right.

items.name: Ep11 Scene 3: Canto X — The Waking (or: How Alice Came Back Different)

sections.Narration:
She knew that Sedna's fingers ARE the sea,
And every seal that feeds a family
Is a gift from someone's wound. She knew
That laughter pulls the sun through, and that two
Clever twins with a SQUASH can beat all Death.
She knew that Coyote wastes his breath
On mischief — and that mischief is the crack
Where new things enter. And they don't come back
The same. She knew that Inanna went
Down naked and returned, and what she spent
Was everything — and what she bought was MORE.
She knew that Elk called wind, that from the floor
Of a primordial sea, a beetle dove
And made the ground. And she knew love
Is sometimes just a spider with a bowl
Who carries fire when the big and whole
And mighty cannot.

Alice closed the book.
She stood. She stretched. She gave the room a look
That rooms don't usually get from little girls:
The look of someone who has seen the curls
Of smoke from Raven's smoke hole, and the gleam
Of Anansi's web, and knows the dream
Below the dream below the dream.

items.name: Ep11 Scene 4: Canto X — The Waking (or: How Alice Came Back Different)

sections.Narration:
'The myths are alive,' she said to no one there.
'The reader is read.'

She straightened her hair.
She put on her shoes.
And she walked out the door
Into a world that was the same as before
Except — it wasn't. Nothing was. Because
She'd been inside the stories, and the CAUSE
Of stories is the oldest thing alive:
The need to say, We're HERE. We survive.
We fell. We made the ground. We lit the flame.
We told the tale. We gave the dark a name.

sections.Note for Grown-Ups: The poems in this grammar are retellings, not replacements. Every story belongs to the tradition that created it. We've linked each poem to its source myth in this repository — please read the originals. They are deeper, stranger, and more alive than any retelling. Alice is a frame; the myths are the picture. When the time is right, we'd love for people from these traditions to read these poems and tell us what we got right and what we got wrong. Until then: we wrote with care, we cited our sources, and we know the difference between visiting a world and owning it.
